items.question: After 146 bce Rome annexed Carthage’s heartland as which grain-exporting province whose amphitheater at El Djem still stands?
items.correctAnswer: The Roman province of Africa (roughly modern Tunisia)
items.explanation: Olive oil and wheat from its coast fed imperial distributions, tying North African estates to Rome’s urban diet.

items.question: Askia Muhammad and Sunni Ali are remembered as expansionist rulers of which Niger-bend empire after Mali’s fragmentation?
incorrectAnswers: The Kingdom of Kongo in 900 ce
items.explanation: Moroccan invasion at Tondibi (1591) ended its riverine hegemony yet left scholarly chronicles in Timbuktu.

items.question: Mortarless stone enclosures and the Zimbabwe Bird carvings symbolize which Shona-linked trading center flourishing c. 1100–1450?
items.explanation: Cattle, crops, and Indian Ocean gold exports sustained a regional power later mythologized by colonial speculators.

items.question: Mūsā I’s 1324 pilgrimage spending reportedly depressed gold prices for years in which Egyptian city?
items.explanation: Chroniclers linked his largesse to Mali’s reputation as a kingdom of almost mythical mineral abundance.

items.question: Northern Ethiopian Aksumite kings minted coins and traded across the Red Sea while rivaling which lower Nile kingdom?
items.explanation: Christianization in the 4th century linked Aksum to Byzantine networks while Arabian wars later eroded its overseas sway.

items.question: Phoenician settlers from Tyre founded which North African thalassocracy that fought Rome in three Punic Wars?
items.explanation: Its agrarian hinterland and ports linked Mediterranean silver with trans-Saharan relays long before Roman Africa.

items.question: Ptolemaic rulers in which Nile entrepôt controlled Upper Egypt and Red Sea trade before Roman annexation in 30 bce?
items.explanation: Its library and lighthouse fame symbolized Hellenistic Africa’s fusion with pharaonic administration.

items.question: Sundiata Keita’s Mande forces broke Susu power and laid foundations for which gold-rich successor to Ghana?
items.explanation: Later rulers like Mūsā I displayed fabulous hajj wealth, projecting Sahelian Islam across the Mediterranean imagination.

items.question: Terracotta heads and ironworking from central Nigeria are associated with which early 1st-millennium bce–ce culture?
items.explanation: Its artistic naturalism intrigues archaeologists studying transitions to complex societies south of the Sahara.

items.question: Which Nubian kingdom south of Egypt grew wealthy controlling Nile trade and briefly ruled Egypt as the 25th Dynasty?
items.explanation: Pyramids at Meroë and royal burials at Kurru illustrate its long dialogue with pharaonic and later Roman power.

items.question: Which Sudanic empire, also called Wagadu, taxed trans-Saharan gold and salt traffic between the 7th and 13th centuries?
incorrectAnswers: The modern Republic of Ghana in 800 ce
items.explanation: Arab chroniclers described ghāna as a royal title while Kumbi Saleh archaeology hints at paired Muslim and royal towns.

items.question: Which semi-legendary founder of Mali appears in the Epic of Sundiata as the unifier of Mande clans against Sumanguru?
items.explanation: Griot performances and Arabic histories alike embed him in Sahelian statecraft memory.

items.question: Bismarck hosted 1884–85 talks in Berlin that accelerated European partition during which land rush?
incorrectAnswers: The Congress of Vienna in Africa
items.explanation: Rules on river navigation and ‘effective occupation’ turned competitive treaty-making into formal empire.

items.question: Cecil Rhodes’s British South Africa Company spearheaded expansion into which territory named for him?
items.explanation: White minority land laws there long outlasted the company charter itself.

items.question: France’s policy of assimilating coastal elites in its sub-Saharan empire was known by what term?
items.explanation: Budget realities and African resistance pushed reforms toward association and broader citizenship debates after 1945.

items.question: Frederick Lugard’s administration of Northern Nigeria codified British rule through local emirs under what doctrine?
items.explanation: He theorized uplift and revenue extraction in The Dual Mandate, influencing chartered and crown colonies alike.

items.question: Leopold II’s International Association gained recognition over the Congo basin before it became which notorious private realm?
items.explanation: Rubber quotas and punitive militias provoked an international reform campaign that forced Belgian annexation.

items.question: Louis Botha and Jan Smuts led which dominion’s forces in seizing German South West Africa during World War I?
items.explanation: Mandate rule after Versailles embedded South African segregationist policy in the territory.

items.question: Portugal claimed which vast territories stitched with forced-labor cotton schemes into the 1960s?
items.explanation: Long wars of liberation there outlasted many British and French decolonization timelines.

items.question: The 1904–07 war of extermination in German South West Africa targeted which pastoral peoples?
items.explanation: Survivors’ testimony fed early international debates about colonial genocide and reparations.

items.question: Which 1897 British punitive expedition looted bronzes from a West African palace city?
items.explanation: Dispersed artworks became flashpoints in contemporary restitution debates.

items.question: Which 1899–1902 conflict pitted British imperial forces against Boer republics rich in Transvaal gold?
items.correctAnswer: The South African War (Second Boer War)
incorrectAnswers: The Maji Maji revolt in the Cape
items.explanation: Concentration camps and guerrilla countermeasures foreshadowed 20th-century colonial warfare’s brutality.

items.question: Which 1905–07 uprising in German East Africa linked spirit mediums to anti-colonial tax resistance?
items.explanation: Drought and scorched-earth repression turned a regional revolt into a demographic catastrophe.

items.question: Which chartered company exploited Nigeria’s palm-oil zones before Lugard’s amalgamation of 1914?
items.correctAnswer: The Royal Niger Company (and allied mercantile interests)
incorrectAnswers: The Dutch East India Company in Lagos
items.explanation: Company flags yielded to crown colony administration as London sought tighter fiscal control.

items.question: Haile Selassie’s 1936 League of Nations speech protested which fascist power’s use of gas in Ethiopia?
items.explanation: Collective security’s failure foreshadowed wider Ethiopian resistance and Allied liberation campaigns.

items.question: Idi Amin’s 1972 expulsion of which minority community reshaped Ugandan trade and diaspora memory?
items.correctAnswer: Ugandan Asians (mostly of South Asian origin)
items.explanation: The decree intersected with Africanization rhetoric and British resettlement offers.

items.question: Jomo Kenyatta’s leadership of which nationalist party helped secure Kenyan independence after the Mau Mau emergency?
items.explanation: Land and loyalist grievances from the emergency complicated postcolonial elite bargains.

items.question: Julius Nyerere’s ujamaa villagization policy was tied to independence in which East African state in 1961?
items.explanation: Union with Zanzibar in 1964 merged socialist experiments with a revolution-toppled Arab oligarchy.

items.question: Menilek II’s 1896 victory at Adwa preserved independence for which African empire against Italy?
items.explanation: European observers had to concede that a chartered African state could defeat a modern expeditionary army.

items.question: Swahili stone towns from Mogadishu to Kilwa traded gold and ivory for Indian cloth under influence from where?
items.correctAnswer: The Indian Ocean monsoon network (Arab, Persian, Indian, and later Portuguese merchants)
items.explanation: Islamic law schools and architectural styles linked these ports to wider monsoon civilization.

items.question: The 1884–85 partition assigned which vast interior realm to Leopold II’s personal association before Belgian takeover?
items.explanation: Rubber quotas and punitive expeditions there became a global scandal by the early 1900s.

items.question: Vasco da Gama’s 1498 arrival on which Kenyan island signaled Portuguese entry into East African waters?
items.correctAnswer: Malindi (with Mombasa contacts on the same voyage)
items.explanation: Pilot Ahmad ibn Mājid’s knowledge helped bridge the Arabian Sea, altering Swahili city diplomacy.

items.question: Which 1994 genocide prompted a French-opposed RPF takeover and massive refugee flows into eastern Zaire?
items.correctAnswer: The Rwandan genocide against Tutsi and moderate Hutu
items.explanation: International tribunals later prosecuted planners, while Great Lakes instability echoed for decades.

items.question: Which Omani sultanate moved its capital to Zanzibar and dominated clove plantations and slave sales in the 19th century?
items.correctAnswer: The sultanate of Zanzibar (Sayyid Saʿīd’s relocation)
items.explanation: British pressure later ended the public slave market while keeping imperial trade interests intact.

items.question: Which Red Sea kingdom’s coins and stelae mark it as a major ivory-and-gold entrepôt opposite Arabia?
incorrectAnswers: Great Zimbabwe on the Red Sea
items.explanation: Its rulers intervened in Yemen before Persian and later Arab expansion clipped overseas ambitions.

items.question: Which island’s 1959–60 revolution ended the Malagasy monarchy before Cold War alignments in the Indian Ocean?
items.explanation: Its unique Austronesian ties and French legacies shaped distinct decolonization debates.

items.question: Ahmed Ben Bella led the FLN to victory ending which settler-dominated French war in 1962?
items.explanation: A million pieds-noirs fled while Algerian nation-building debated socialism and Arab identity.

items.question: Haile Selassie’s 1974 overthrow opened which brutal Ethiopian revolutionary era named for a committee?
items.explanation: Famine, Red Terror purges, and eventually Tigrayan-led rebellion reshaped the Horn for decades.

items.question: Ian Smith’s 1965 UDI attempted to preserve white minority rule in which British-linked territory?
items.explanation: Guerrilla war and sanctions eventually yielded majority rule as Zimbabwe in 1980.

items.question: Julius Nyerere and Milton Obote were early presidents tied to independence in which British East African neighbors?
items.explanation: East African Community experiments previewed regional integration hopes—and jealousies.

items.question: Kwame Nkrumah’s Convention People’s Party won self-government first for which 1957 British-ruled state?
items.correctAnswer: Ghana (from the Gold Coast and British Togoland)
items.explanation: Its independence became a psychological ‘first’ for tropical Africa’s decolonization wave.

items.question: Nelson Mandela’s 1990 release preceded multiracial elections ending apartheid in which year?
items.explanation: The ANC’s victory embedded transitional justice debates that still shape South African politics.

items.question: Patrice Lumumba’s premiership collapsed into crisis after which 1960 Congo independence week mutiny?
items.correctAnswer: The Force Publique mutiny and ensuing international intervention
items.explanation: UN peacekeepers, secession in Katanga, and Cold War intrigue framed Africa’s first great postcolonial trauma.

items.question: Which 1963 founding charter organization based in Addis Ababa promoted continental unity against colonialism?
items.explanation: It later morphed into the African Union while retaining borders inherited from Berlin’s partition.

items.question: Which 1967–70 secession war saw Biafra challenge Nigeria’s federal military government?
items.explanation: Starvation imagery in global media transformed humanitarian NGOs’ role in African conflicts.

items.question: Which 1990 Namibian independence followed a UN transition plan after South African withdrawal?
items.explanation: SWAPO’s electoral win capped one of Africa’s longest decolonization struggles.

items.question: Which 1993 vote backed Eritrean separation from Ethiopia after decades of armed struggle?
items.explanation: Initial cooperation soured into the devastating 1998–2000 border war.

items.question: Which southern African territory’s 1975 independence followed armed struggle by MPLA, UNITA, and FNLA after Lisbon’s 1974 coup?
items.explanation: Cuban intervention and South African raids turned decolonization into a long Cold War battlefield.

items.question: After Actium, which queen’s joint rule with Antony ended in defeat near Alexandria in 30 bce?
items.explanation: Egypt’s annexation as a Roman province made the Nile valley central to imperial grain supply.

items.question: Erwin Rommel’s Afrika Korps fought Commonwealth forces across Egypt and which plateau in 1941–43?
items.explanation: El Alamein became shorthand for the turn from Axis advance toward Allied Mediterranean offensives.

items.question: Italy’s 1911–12 seizure of Tripoli and Cyrenaica from the Ottomans created which colony?
items.explanation: Resistance under Sanūsī leadership later intersected with World War I desert campaigns.

items.question: Phoenician Tyre founded which Punic rival of Rome on the Gulf of Tunis?
items.explanation: Punic Wars ended in 146 bce destruction, yet Roman Carthage later revived as an imperial metropolis.

items.question: Tariq ibn Ziyad’s 711 crossing gave which Arabic name to the strait facing Gibraltar?
items.explanation: Umayyad armies ended Visigothic rule and began centuries of Al-Andalus civilization.

items.question: The 1979 Egypt-Israel peace treaty followed which earlier war that saw Egyptian forces cross the Suez Canal?
items.explanation: Petroleum politics and superpower détente framed the conflict’s global echoes.

items.question: The Almohads and Almoravids built empires bridging which desert-edge region and Andalusian Islamicate culture?
items.explanation: Reformist doctrines mobilized Sanhājah warriors into states that reshaped trans-Saharan jurisprudence.

items.question: Which 1830 French expedition began the long conquest of Ottoman Algiers’ beylik?
items.explanation: Settler colonization and ‘civilizing’ ideology there fueled resistance from Abd al-Qādir to the FLN.

items.question: Which 1898 confrontation at a Sudanese Nile fort nearly brought Britain and France to war over African claims?
items.explanation: Diplomatic climb-down produced the Entente Cordiale’s African bargains.

items.question: Which 1956 decision to nationalize the Suez Canal triggered an Anglo-French-Israeli attack on Egypt?
items.explanation: U.S. financial pressure forced a humiliating withdrawal, boosting pan-Arab prestige.

items.question: Which 2010–11 uprising ousted Zine el-Abidine Ben Ali and helped ignite wider Arab Spring protests?
incorrectAnswers: The Algerian October 1988 events replayed alone
items.explanation: Youth unemployment and police brutality fused with social media to spread tactics across the region.

items.question: Which Numidian king allied with Rome against Carthage in the Second Punic War yet later clashed with the Republic?
items.explanation: His long reign encouraged settled farming on the Tell, shifting power balances in the Maghrib.

items.question: Arab writers called which Soninke-led empire ‘Ghāna’ while its rulers used the name Wagadu?
items.explanation: Control of gold coming north and salt coming south made its capitals magnets for Muslim merchants.

items.question: Sunni ʿAlī’s campaigns from Gao built which empire that later fell to Moroccan muskets at Tondibi?
items.explanation: River fleets and cavalry let it dominate middle Niger towns before firearm asymmetry ended autonomy.

items.question: The 1885 Berlin West Africa Conference accelerated European claims during which continent-wide land rush?
items.explanation: Rules on ‘effective occupation’ turned sketchy treaties into color-coded colonial maps.

items.question: The ‘Atlantic system’ drew millions of captives from which broad region between Senegambia and Angola?
items.correctAnswer: West and West-Central Africa (including the Slave Coast and Angola routes)
items.explanation: Forts like Elmina organized human cargoes whose profits linked African elites to European shippers.

items.question: Usman dan Fodio’s jihads created which 19th-century caliphate centered on Sokoto in today’s northern Nigeria?
items.explanation: Its emirates blended Maliki law with Hausa administration, reshaping Sahelian Islamicate politics.

items.question: Which 18th-century Asantehene is credited with crushing Denkyera and enthroning kings on the Golden Stool at Kumasi?
items.explanation: His wars fused Akan towns into a forest empire that traded captives for firearms with Atlantic forts.

items.question: Which Afro-Brazilian trader repatriated to Lagos helped spread Christianity and commerce in 19th-century Yorubaland?
items.explanation: Freed from a slave ship as a youth, he rose to Anglican bishopric while documenting Niger missions.

items.question: Which Akan forest state supplied gold that fed trans-Saharan and Atlantic trades before British annexation?
items.explanation: Golden Stool ideology and Kumasi diplomacy repeatedly challenged coastal British governors.

items.question: Which Edo kingdom south of the Niger became famous for brass plaques and long-distance trade with Europeans?
items.explanation: Palace art chronicled court hierarchy while Atlantic exchanges reshaped regional economies.

items.question: Which Mali emperor’s hajj spending in Cairo advertised Sahelian gold to the wider Islamic world in 1324?
items.explanation: His caravan’s scale became a medieval benchmark for African wealth in Arabic chronicles.

items.question: Which Niger-bend city’s Sankore mosque became a famed manuscript hub under Mali and Songhai patronage?
items.explanation: Caravans linked desert-edge scholarship with Mediterranean book markets and royal courts.

items.question: Which Volta-region group built earthwork capitals and resisted expansion by Asante and Europeans into the 19th century?
items.correctAnswer: The Fante (alongside other coastal Akan polities)
items.explanation: Coastal confederations negotiated with the Royal Navy while maintaining caravan links inland.
